dating.display: proclaimed 2003; inscribed on the Representative List 2008
dating.basis: UNESCO's own proclamation and inscription records.

summary: UNESCO proclaimed the tradition of Vedic chanting a Masterpiece of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity (2003) and inscribed it on the Representative List (2008) — recognizing the pāṭha recitation systems (pada, krama, jaṭā, ghana) still transmitted teacher-to-student in living śākhās, the same error-detecting oral architecture that carried the Ṛgveda across roughly three millennia.
whyItMatters: It closes the atlas's arc: the transmission system documented in the Vedic-era entries is not an archaeological memory but a living practice with international heritage status — deep time arriving intact in the present.
honestyNote: Recognition is of the chanting tradition's cultural value, not an endorsement of any historical claim about the texts' dates or content; UNESCO's records also note the tradition's fragility (few full śākhā lineages survive).

sources.label: UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage: Tradition of Vedic Chanting (inscription record)
Staal, F., "The Fidelity of Oral Tradition and the Origins of Science" (1986)
Jamison, S. & Brereton, J., The Rigveda (OUP, 2014), introduction — on the recitation systems
Vedic Heritage Portal (IGNCA) — the national documentation behind the UNESCO inscription

image.alt: Homa/yajña fire ritual — the living Vedic ritual context

dharmicTie.statement: The śruti transmission system itself, alive: the pāṭha architecture the śikṣā vedāṅga codifies, still taught mouth to ear in unbroken śākhā lineages.

scriptureRefs.text: the vedāṅga of correct recitation — the theory behind the practice UNESCO inscribed
